.ddx/beads.jsonl:265:{"acceptance":"1. 5 fixture beads chosen and listed in .ddx/executions/\u003crun-id\u003e/comparison-fixtures.md with: bead ID, base-rev, original closing commit, characterization (trivial/refactor/test/multi-file/docs).\n2. Path A run: each fixture's bead replayed via ddx try (or ddx work --once) on a branch from base-rev~1; outcomes captured.\n3. Path B run: each fixture dispatched via Agent tool with bead's auto-prompt verbatim; outcomes captured.\n4. Path C run: each fixture dispatched via Agent tool with operator hand-curated prompt; outcomes captured.\n5. Per-fixture, per-path measurements table with the 7 named metrics.\n6. Aggregate stats + failure-mode distribution.\n7. Hypothesis verdict section: H1 / H2 / both / inconclusive.\n8. Recommendations section: which path for which bead-shape.\n9. Report committed at .ddx/executions/\u003crun-id\u003e/ddx-vs-subagent-comparison-2026-05-03.md.\n10. Conventional commit ending [\u003cthis-bead-id\u003e].\n11. lefthook run pre-commit passes.\n\nDEPS (gate execution; bead stays open until these close): refactor children C4-C13 (ddx-358f2457, ddx-c8f79963, ddx-b669bb9f, ddx-06eb05d8, ddx-848069a3, ddx-9228a484, ddx-0b8780db, ddx-c670ef0a, ddx-387a0178).","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:41.880567652Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-358f2457","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-c8f79963","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-b669bb9f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-06eb05d8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-848069a3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-9228a484","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-0b8780db","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-c670ef0a","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-31f745cd","depends_on_id":"ddx-387a0178","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-03T19:55:42Z"}],"description":"Hand-curated sub-agent prompts have been outperforming ddx work / ddx try in this session. Two competing hypotheses for why:\nH1: ddx execution machinery (the 9 fail-prone layers) is unreliable — failure is in the workers/server code.\nH2: Bead authoring quality is insufficient — beads punt investigation; hand-curation papers over the gap.\n\nBoth are plausible. Determining the relative contribution requires a 3-path study, NOT the original 2-path proposal.\n\nTHREE PATHS\n\nPath A: ddx try / ddx work with the bead's auto-prompt verbatim (current production path).\nPath B: Sub-agent dispatched with the bead's auto-prompt verbatim (NOT hand-curated). Same prompt as A; different executor. Isolates ddx machinery from prompt quality.\nPath C: Sub-agent dispatched with operator hand-curated prompt (file:line cites, named tests, explicit non-scope, etc.). Establishes the ceiling.\n\nINTERPRETATION\n- A vs B differ → ddx machinery is the failure (file follow-ups against the 9 layers).\n- B vs C differ → bead authoring quality is the failure (already covered by bead-quality-audit + retrofit).\n- A == B \u003c\u003c C → both are real, with bead quality being the dominant gap.\n\nMETHOD\nPick 5 representative CLOSED beads as fixtures: one trivial fix, one refactor child, one test addition, one multi-file feature, one documentation update. For each fixture, run all 3 paths on a branch from the bead's base-rev (parent of the original work commit). Throwaway commits — the original work is already in main. We're measuring path properties, not producing mergeable code.\n\nMEASUREMENTS\nPer path per bead:\n- Time-to-first-claim (A) / time-to-first-output (B,C)\n- Time-to-merge-eligible-commit (whether or not we merge)\n- Attempts (A) / iterations (B,C)\n- Status outcomes: success / no_changes / preserved / blocked / errored\n- Cost: tokens, USD\n- Manual interventions\n- Failure mode classified per the 9-layer machinery list\n\nOUTPUT\n.ddx/executions/\u003crun-id\u003e/ddx-vs-subagent-comparison-2026-05-03.md with:\n- Per-bead per-path table\n- Aggregate stats (median + p99)\n- Failure-mode distribution\n- Hypothesis verdict (H1 vs H2 vs both)\n- Recommendations for which path is appropriate for which bead-shape\n\nTIMING\nThis study runs AFTER the executeloop refactor (C0-C13) lands so the ddx machinery is its post-refactor shape. Running pre-refactor measures behavior we're about to change. RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES + bead-quality-audit + retrofit + refactor must all complete first. This bead is filed now and EXECUTION DEFERRED.\n\nNOT IN SCOPE\n- Fixing whatever the study reveals (separate bug beads filed from the study's findings)\n- Running against pre-refactor ddx machinery (data we won't act on)\n- Comparing different sub-agent harnesses (different concern)\n\nEVIDENCE\n- ddx-f339c399 documents the 2026-05-04 ddx try ddx-29058e2a --harness=codex no_changes outcome and is partial Path B-vs-C data favoring the bead-quality hypothesis.","id":"ddx-31f745cd","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2","area:agent","area:tooling","kind:study","reliability","bead-quality"],"notes":"Partial Path B-vs-C evidence: ddx-f339c399 documents the 2026-05-04 ddx try ddx-29058e2a --harness=codex no_changes outcome, supporting the bead-quality hypothesis side of the study.","parent":"ddx-e34994e2","priority":2,"status":"open","title":"study: 3-path execution comparison (ddx work vs sub-agent + verbatim bead vs sub-agent + hand-curated)","updated_at":"2026-05-05T03:08:30.860761453Z"}

.ddx/beads.jsonl:282:{"acceptance":"FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-14T05:16:15Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1412283","closing_commit_sha":"77ac255569b6a9d82302bec4969160a538a70ca1","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:16:05.174041066Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-359706d2","depends_on_id":"ddx-ffda4fb5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:20:18Z"}],"description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec work\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one .ddx/beads.jsonl:418:{"acceptance":"FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:14:49.010697815Z","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec work\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one Find them and either:\n- File unification beads following the ExecuteLoopSpec pattern, OR\n- Document them as already-clean (and why).\n\nCOMMANDS TO AUDIT (per codex review — expanded)\n\n1. ddx agent run (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go ~395-414 cobra flags) PLUS its server endpoint /api/agent/run (cli/internal/server/server.go:2150-2188) AND its MCP equivalent (cli/internal/server/server.go:4537-4553). The server endpoint accepts only Harness/Prompt/Model/Effort while cobra has many more flags — same drop risk as ExecuteLoop, narrower fields.\n2. ddx work passthrough (alias for ddx agent execute-loop with implicit OpaquePassthrough — covered by ddx-29058e2a but verify the implicit behavior is documented in the spec)\n3. GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter (cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137) — covered by ddx-29058e2a explicitly, but audit any OTHER GraphQL adapters with similar patterns\n4. Server-side worker variants in cli/internal/server/workers.go — anything else that takes a Spec struct\n5. ddx bead create / update — flag set is small but check whether server endpoint mirrors\n6. ddx server commands (ddx server workers list/show/log) — read-only, less risk but verify\n7. ddx try — NOT shipped as a cobra command in this tree. Record as N/A; do not chase.\n8. ddx run — same: if not shipped, N/A.\n\nAUDIT METHOD (per codex — generated diff, not gut-feel)\n\nFor each command:\n1. Enumerate cobra flag list (programmatic: parse cobra command struct or grep for `cmd.Flags().*Var`).\n2. Enumerate corresponding server JSON request struct fields (programmatic: AST parse the request struct in handler).\n3. Enumerate the spec/persisted struct fields (programmatic).\n4. Enumerate the final consumer fields (programmatic).\n5. Diff each layer's field set; classify each field as: execution / control-plane / output-only / unsupported / dropped.\n6. Drop-risk score: count of fields that exist in cobra but NOT in subsequent layers.\n\nOUTPUT\n\nReport at .ddx/executions/\u003crun-id\u003e/parallel-struct-audit.md:\n- One section per command audited\n- For each: layer count, list of fields per layer, classification table, drop-risk score, recommendation (unify / clean / N/A)\n- Summary table: command | layer count | drop-risk count | recommendation\n\nIf unification candidates found, file follow-up beads using the ExecuteLoopSpec refactor (ddx-29058e2a) as the template.\n\nDEP CHANGE (per codex)\n- DEP on ddx-29058e2a is RELAXED to \"may begin read-only command discovery + diff generation in parallel; only the implementation-pattern recommendations need ddx-29058e2a as reference.\" Update dep semantics so this can start now.","id":"ddx-fb290074","issue_type":"task","labels":["phase:2","area:agent","area:server","kind:audit","prevention"],"parent":"ddx-e34994e2","priority":1,"status":"open","title":"audit: parallel-struct smell — find other ddx commands with multi-layer flag wiring","updated_at":"2026-05-03T00:16:25.804859332Z"}

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1291:{"acceptance":"SD-015 defines a stable model-level attribution key for routing metrics so RoutingOutcome, QuotaSnapshot, and any derived burn/performance summaries can be associated with the resolved candidate model or canonical target; FEAT-014 stays consistent with that key; and the docs no longer imply that latency/cost/reliability from different models on the same surface are mixed into one routing signal","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T03:46:32Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"327749","closing_commit_sha":"0364b777bb79faf9eeef12a4d940b190d687c894","created_at":"2026-04-10T03:45:04.557331352Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md:175\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new minimal-routing schema records `RoutingOutcome`, `QuotaSnapshot`, and `BurnSummary` only by `harness` and `surface`. Earlier in the same design, however, each `CandidatePlan` carries `CanonicalTarget`, `ConcreteModel`, `EstimatedCostUSD`, and `PerformanceMetrics` because routing decisions can differ materially by resolved model or exact pin. A single surface can host very different models with different latency, reliability, and cost characteristics. If DDx aggregates outcome and burn data only at the harness/surface level, runs for one model will contaminate routing signals for another, making model-specific scoring and exact-pin behavior incorrect.\nSuggested fix: Extend the minimal routing-metrics schema with a model-identifying dimension such as `canonical_target` or `concrete_model` (or explicitly define an equivalent normalization key), and update FEAT-014/SD-015 so performance, cost, and burn inputs remain attributable to the resolved model rather than only the surface.","id":"hx-4a12a619","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:agent","area:docs"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: keep routing metrics model-specific","updated_at":"2026-04-10T03:47:24.607997607Z"}

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1332:{"acceptance":"SD-016 states that cost_usd=-1 means unknown rather than known cost, direct cost aggregation excludes the sentinel value, and the cost_state / cost precedence rules align with FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 sentinel semantics","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T23:00:02Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2091135","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:35:00.80036448Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md:191\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: high\nDescription: SD-016 defines `cost_state=known` whenever a session row reports `cost_usd` and says bead lifecycle cost should use `session.cost_usd` whenever it is present. But FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 explicitly define `cost_usd=-1` as the sentinel for unknown cost, not a real price. Implementing SD-016 as written would treat unknown-cost sessions as known values, producing negative dollar totals and skipping the estimation/unknown fallback for a core FEAT-016 metric.\nSuggested fix: Update SD-016 so `cost_usd=-1` is mapped to `cost_state=unknown` and excluded from direct cost sums, with estimation or unknown fallback applied only to non-sentinel values.","id":"hx-72b9a368","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:high"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: treat cost_usd=-1 as unknown in SD-016","updated_at":"2026-04-10T00:21:23.11757511Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:628:{"acceptance":"1. .ddx/config.yaml schema supports profile_ladders (per-profile ordered tier list) AND the legacy profile_priority is still readable with a deprecation warning.\n\n2. Four shipped profile ladders: default=[cheap,standard,smart], cheap=[cheap], fast=[fast,smart], smart=[smart]. Documented in FEAT-006.\n\n3. Tier escalation in routing.go / execute_bead_orchestrator.go iterates profile_ladders[P] on failure, respecting --provider affinity (ddx-3c5ba7cc) and capability gating (ddx-4817edfd). Escalation triggers: execution_failed, land_conflict, post_run_check_failed, structural_validation_failed. no_changes uses the existing cooldown path.\n\n4. Bead routing event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. A run that escalates cheap -\u003e standard -\u003e success at standard has an event with escalation_count=1, resolved_tier=standard, final_tier=standard.\n\n5. 'ddx work --profile default' drains the queue by trying local first and escalating only when needed. Measured outcome over a test run of 10 representative beads shows at least 50% closing at the cheap tier (target: most work lands locally; escalations are the exception).\n\n6. Regression tests cover each ladder path: success-at-cheap, escalate-cheap-to-standard, escalate-all-tiers-then-exhaustion, --max-tier caps the ladder, --profile cheap never escalates.\n\n7. FEAT-006 Profile Routing section updated with the ladder semantics and the intent table (default/cheap/fast/smart).","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"93f938c41c93be78fc199bd150f455add6575d24","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:51:49.512908486Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-2d974641","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T02:52:01Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-e7254128","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T13:58:09Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-7bc0c8d5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-20T14:36:21Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-bbb65768","depends_on_id":"ddx-ea7f041c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:36:16Z"}],"description":"## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. .ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:934:{"acceptance":"SD-015 defines a stable model-level attribution key for routing metrics so RoutingOutcome, QuotaSnapshot, and any derived burn/performance summaries can be associated with the resolved candidate model or canonical target; FEAT-014 stays consistent with that key; and the docs no longer imply that latency/cost/reliability from different models on the same surface are mixed into one routing signal","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T03:46:32Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"327749","closing_commit_sha":"0364b777bb79faf9eeef12a4d940b190d687c894","created_at":"2026-04-10T03:45:04.557331352Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md:175\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new minimal-routing schema records `RoutingOutcome`, `QuotaSnapshot`, and `BurnSummary` only by `harness` and `surface`. Earlier in the same design, however, each `CandidatePlan` carries `CanonicalTarget`, `ConcreteModel`, `EstimatedCostUSD`, and `PerformanceMetrics` because routing decisions can differ materially by resolved model or exact pin. A single surface can host very different models with different latency, reliability, and cost characteristics. If DDx aggregates outcome and burn data only at the harness/surface level, runs for one model will contaminate routing signals for another, making model-specific scoring and exact-pin behavior incorrect.\nSuggested fix: Extend the minimal routing-metrics schema with a model-identifying dimension such as `canonical_target` or `concrete_model` (or explicitly define an equivalent normalization key), and update FEAT-014/SD-015 so performance, cost, and burn inputs remain attributable to the resolved model rather than only the surface.","id":"hx-4a12a619","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:agent","area:docs"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-agent-routing-and-catalog-resolution.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: keep routing metrics model-specific","updated_at":"2026-04-10T03:47:24.607997607Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:975:{"acceptance":"SD-016 states that cost_usd=-1 means unknown rather than known cost, direct cost aggregation excludes the sentinel value, and the cost_state / cost precedence rules align with FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 sentinel semantics","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T23:00:02Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2091135","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:35:00.80036448Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md:191\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: high\nDescription: SD-016 defines `cost_state=known` whenever a session row reports `cost_usd` and says bead lifecycle cost should use `session.cost_usd` whenever it is present. But FEAT-014 and FEAT-006 explicitly define `cost_usd=-1` as the sentinel for unknown cost, not a real price. Implementing SD-016 as written would treat unknown-cost sessions as known values, producing negative dollar totals and skipping the estimation/unknown fallback for a core FEAT-016 metric.\nSuggested fix: Update SD-016 so `cost_usd=-1` is mapped to `cost_state=unknown` and excluded from direct cost sums, with estimation or unknown fallback applied only to non-sentinel values.","id":"hx-72b9a368","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:high"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: treat cost_usd=-1 as unknown in SD-016","updated_at":"2026-04-10T00:21:23.11757511Z"}

.ddx/executions/20260411T165740-77adba47/manifest.json:12:    "description": "\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nUpdate the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.\n\n## Goals\n- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics\n- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database\n- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later\n- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan\n- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue\n\n## Boundaries\n- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work\n- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",

.ddx/executions/20260412T225813-52591a3a/manifest.json:13:    "description": "\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: execute-bead prompt rationalization, tracked execution evidence, and commit provenance. .ddx/attachments/ddx-e08238ca/events.jsonl:2:{"actor":"opus-reviewer","body":"Opus review of 6910c2e.\n\nEXECUTIONEVIDENCE: resolver_sub_exec.go:39-90 polls r.ExecLogs.GetExecLog(runID) every 500ms. Adapter at graphql_adapters.go:13-16 delegates to ddxexec.NewStore(workingDir).Log(runID) reading real run bundle. When available, splits by newlines, emits each as ExecutionEvent with seq-based EventID, then closes channel. ONE-SHOT dump (waits for run completion, then streams all lines at once) — not live tail. Acceptable given schema name is \"executionEvidence\" (final artifact), not \"executionTail\". Limitation worth documenting upstream.\n\nCOORDINATORMETRICS: resolver_sub_exec.go:96-150 polls CoordMetrics.GetCoordinatorMetrics(projectRoot) every MetricsPollInterval (default 1s). Adapter at graphql_adapters.go:24-37 calls reg.MetricsFor(projectRoot) on real workers.LandCoordinators registry. Diff with prev via snap != last, emits only on change. 1s interval appropriate for land events (seconds-to-minutes timescale).\n\nBOTH TESTS REAL: httptest.NewServer + full graphql-transport-ws handshake. Coordinator test uses atomic.Pointer[CoordinatorMetricsSnap] flipped mid-test to simulate registry updates — verifies diff emission across snap1 -> snap2.\n\nGRAPHQL_ADAPTERS.GO: proper import-cycle workaround. graphql package defines ExecLogProvider/CoordinatorMetricsProvider interfaces + CoordinatorMetricsSnap DTO; outer server package provides concrete implementations (execLogAdapter, coordMetricsAdapter). Standard pattern for nested resolver wiring.\n\nMINOR (non-blocking):\n1. executionEvidence polls forever until ctx cancels if run never completes — correct but worth docs\n2. 3 separate test helpers (newGraphQLHandlerWithBeadBus/ExecLogs/CoordMetrics) duplicate ~90 lines of setup — cosmetic dedupe opportunity (note in refactor bead)\n\nVERDICT: ACCEPT.","created_at":"2026-04-15T01:05:22.694240511Z","kind":"review","source":"opus batch review (Stage 2.11 + 2.12)","summary":"ACCEPT: both subscriptions poll real data sources with diff-based emission. executionEvidence streams real ddxexec.Store log once after run completes (500ms poll for availability); coordinatorMetrics polls real workers.LandCoordinators registry every 1s with snapshot diff. Both tests use full graphql-transport-ws WebSocket handshakes. graphql_adapters.go is proper import-cycle workaround."}

.ddx/executions/20260413T204522-89293e6f/manifest.json:15:    "description": "\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nUpdate the governing server planning docs so they describe the current intended host+user ddx-server model before follow-on server design and implementation beads continue.\n\n## Goals\n- Reconcile FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 around a single host+user ddx-server instance with explicit project registry, worker management boundaries, and queue semantics\n- Define the split between git-backed project truth, tracked execution evidence, and host-local runtime/index state backed by an embedded database\n- Define the current transport/security model: localhost unauthenticated HTTP for UI/API now, explicit future transport/auth evolution later\n- Add the embedded-agent progress, provider dashboard, replay-backed fixture, and Playwright coverage requirements to the governed server plan\n- Make the scheduling boundary explicit: server may allocate workers across projects, but bead ordering remains owned by each project queue\n\n## Boundaries\n- Keep Axon and multi-host service-backed topology as later-stage work\n- Do not reopen the already-shipped single-project execute-bead loop contract except where the new server plan must reference it",
.ddx/executions/20260413T204522-89293e6f/manifest.json:16:    "acceptance": "FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, and TP-002 all describe the same host+user ddx-server model, including runtime/index storage, worker boundaries, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and mandatory Playwright coverage for new UI surfaces",
.ddx/executions/20260413T204522-89293e6f/prompt.md:17:Review area: host+user ddx-server planning stack after the server model evolved beyond the earlier multi-project topology draft. Evidence covers FEAT-002, FEAT-008, FEAT-013, FEAT-014, SD-019, TP-002, the fresh-eyes server review, and the updated requirements for a per-user host daemon, localhost HTTP UI, SQLite-backed runtime/index state, embedded-agent progress visibility, provider dashboarding, replay-backed fixtures, and Playwright coverage for every new UI surface.

.ddx/executions/20260413T214907-a42abbf6/manifest.json:14:    "description": "\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nReview area: server agent observability and operator controls. Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nDefine the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.\n\n## Goals\n- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known\n- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps\n- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window\n- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values\n\n## Required spec work\n- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed\n- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces\n- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty\n\n## Required implementation planning\n- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states\n- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page",
.ddx/executions/20260413T214907-a42abbf6/manifest.json:15:    "acceptance": "FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 define an agent dashboard showing configured providers, availability/health, utilization metrics, freshness, and unknown-state semantics; the design includes a queryable read model and deterministic fixture scenarios for Playwright coverage",

Evidence covers FEAT-002 server surfaces, FEAT-008 web UI, FEAT-014 token-awareness/routing metrics, the embedded-agent progress lane, and the requirement that a host+user ddx-server expose actionable provider availability and utilization state across configured harnesses/providers.
</context-digest>
Define the server-side agent dashboard that lets operators inspect configured providers and make informed execution decisions.

## Goals
- Show configured providers/harnesses, effective routing availability, auth/health state, and current quota or headroom when known
- Show utilization and performance signals already normalized by DDx: token usage, cost when available, recent latency/success, burn estimates, and freshness timestamps
- Support search/sort/filter/reporting across provider, model, profile, status, and time window
- Distinguish provider-native facts from DDx-derived estimates and from unknown values

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-002, FEAT-008, and FEAT-014 so the dashboard and its backing read model are explicitly governed
- Define the API/read-model fields exposed to UI consumers and reporting surfaces
- Clarify how live provider availability, cached quota snapshots, and historical usage are presented without inventing certainty

## Required implementation planning
- Define sample/fixture data needed to Playwright-test the dashboard states
- Define how the dashboard relates to worker progress, execution history, and routing diagnostics without collapsing them into one page

.ddx/executions/20260425T105546-e5825bd5/prompt.md:5:Stage F1 of FEAT-022. Emit the evidence-assembly telemetry block onto
attempt bundles for review and grading attempts. Add a compact summary
to bead event bodies for review, review-error, and compare-result.

Implements FEAT-022 requirements §15 and §16. Satisfies the event-body
criterion of US-220 and the per-field criterion of US-221.

Depends on Stage C1 (ddx-12934d66) and Stage C2 (ddx-0bbf8563) —
F1 serializes the EvidenceAssemblySection struct pinned in Stage A1,
which C1 and C2 populate during assembly.

In-scope files:
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go: during and after
  assembly, collect per-section EvidenceAssemblySection records
  (struct shape pinned in Stage A1). Write them into result.json /
  manifest.json under an evidence_assembly key (additive to FEAT-014
  §19/§20 runtime-metrics block; does not replace).
- cli/internal/agent/grade.go: same for grading attempts.
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go: on the review and
  review-error events emitted by the loop, populate event body fields
  input_bytes, output_bytes, elapsed_ms, harness, model. Respect the
  existing event-body size cap at cli/internal/bead/store.go:747;
  never include section detail in the event body (that lives only on
  the artifact).
- Similar event-body update for compare-result events emitted by the
  grading flow.

Out-of-scope:
- CLI metrics surface extension — separate Stage F2 bead.
- Any UI or dashboard consumer of the new fields.
- Primary execute-bead event telemetry mirroring (explicitly OOS per
  FEAT-022 Out of Scope).

Rollback: revert telemetry writes; attempt bundles and event bodies
return to prior shape. Artifact-schema compatibility: new key on
result.json is additive; consumers that don't know about it continue
to work.

.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Target UX\n\nThe user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:\n\n### default profile (the common case)\nTry local models first, escalate to cloud if:\n- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt\n- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)\n- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)\n- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)\n\nEscalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.\n\n### cheap profile\nLocal only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.\n\n### fast profile\nCloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.\n\n### smart profile\nCloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.\n\n## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)\n\nAs of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).\n\n## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)\n\nThis epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:\n\n- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation\n- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs\n- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics\n- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead\n\nWith those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.\n\n## Scope\n\n### Config schema additions\n\n.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:\n\n    agent:\n      routing:\n        profile_ladders:\n          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate\n          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only\n          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart\n          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation\n        model_overrides:\n          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6\n          fast:     kimi/k2.5\n          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality\n          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7\n\nCurrent profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.\n\n### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)\n\nFor a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:\n- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).\n- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.\n- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.\n\n### User-facing CLI\n\n- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.\n- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.\n- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).\n\n### Telemetry + measurement\n\nEvery execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.\n\n## Exit gate\n\n'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:\n- Claims the bead.\n- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.\n- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.\n- If standard fails, retries at smart.\n- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.\n- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.\n- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.\n\n## Metadata\n\nGoverning spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.",
.ddx/executions/20260421T163240-eb3cb31f/prompt.md:5:## Target UX

The user-facing profile vocabulary (cheap / default / fast / smart) should map to concrete routing policies that take full advantage of local models without blocking on their limits. Specifically:

### default profile (the common case)
Try local models first, escalate to cloud if:
- local model's context window is too small for the bead prompt
- local model lacks a capability the bead requires (tools, structured output, etc.)
- local dispatch fails (provider unreachable, timeout, model-side error)
- bead reopens due to post-run check failure (review-malfunction, land-conflict, etc.)

Escalation target: next tier up in the ladder (standard cloud — e.g. codex gpt-5.4 within subscription). If THAT fails, escalate once more to smart cloud. Then stop and unclaim.

### cheap profile
Local only. Never escalate. Intended for high-volume exploratory work where cost dominates and adequate-quality is sufficient. If the cheap tier can't serve the bead, the bead unclaims and waits.

### fast profile
Cloud-first, skip local entirely. Intended for latency-critical or interactive work where local inference's 9s warmup is unacceptable. Starts at a fast cloud model (e.g. gemini-flash or haiku) and escalates to smart on failure.

### smart profile
Cloud-high-quality from the start. Intended for bead-review, adversarial checks, or beads whose AC demands high-quality reasoning. No escalation — if smart fails, the work fails meaningfully.

## Why this matters (dogfooding signal)

As of 2026-04-18, DDx's default is cheap = qwen/qwen3.6 local (vidar-omlx). With v0.3.14 shipping the SSE-comment-frame filter, the local path actually works end-to-end. The queue can drain mostly on local hardware for pennies per bead, escalating only when the bead genuinely needs cloud quality. That's the throughput-per-dollar goal (goal:throughput-per-dollar label).

## Prereqs (already filed under ddx-2d974641 or sibling beads)

This epic is blocked on the autorouting epic delivering its exit-gate UX — 'declare provider + use canonical ref + autorouting works'. Specifically:

- ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) — foundation
- ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization + per-provider) — lets cheap-tier model refs resolve to provider-native IDs
- ddx-2f5a2284 (catalog/Profile discovery integration) — lets Profile tier overrides retarget to provider-native IDs
- ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier escalation --provider affinity) — the lower half of this epic's 'escalate on failure' semantics
- ddx-4817edfd (capability gating) — prevents routing to a model that can't serve the bead

With those in place, the profile-level policy here is mostly CONFIGURATION + a small amount of scoring-layer logic.

## Scope

### Config schema additions

.ddx/config.yaml agent.routing extends with an explicit per-profile ladder:

    agent:
      routing:
        profile_ladders:
          default: [cheap, standard, smart]      # local first, escalate
          cheap:   [cheap]                        # local only
          fast:    [fast, smart]                  # cloud fast, escalate to smart
          smart:   [smart]                        # cloud high-quality, no escalation
        model_overrides:
          cheap:    qwen/qwen3.6
          fast:     kimi/k2.5
          standard: codex/gpt-5.4                # new: subscription cloud, mid-quality
          smart:    minimax/minimax-m2.7

Current profile_priority field (a flat list) becomes a legacy alias for profile_ladders.default with a deprecation warning.

### Tier-escalation policy (routing.go)

For a given profile P, iterate profile_ladders[P]:
- Evaluate model_overrides[tier] resolves on a viable candidate (with --provider affinity from ddx-3c5ba7cc).
- If yes, try it; on failure (execution_failed / land_conflict / post_run_check_failed), move to next tier.
- Record tier decision + escalation chain in routing event on the bead so telemetry reflects where work actually landed.

### User-facing CLI

- ddx work --profile default — the new primary invocation. Most users run this.
- ddx work --profile cheap / fast / smart — opt-in deviations.
- --max-tier continues to cap the ladder (explicit user override beats config).

### Telemetry + measurement

Every execution event records: requested_profile, requested_tier, resolved_tier, escalation_count, final_tier. Aggregated metrics (FEAT-016 / ddx-4b59b816) report per-profile throughput, per-tier cost, per-bead escalation rate.

## Exit gate

'ddx work --profile default --once --local' against a ready bead on a host with a healthy local provider AND healthy cloud fallback:
- Claims the bead.
- Tries cheap tier (local). If it succeeds, closes bead with tier=cheap recorded.
- If cheap fails for any escalation-triggering reason, automatically retries at standard tier without operator intervention.
- If standard fails, retries at smart.
- Final outcome recorded faithfully (success at whatever tier landed, or exhaustion if all tiers failed).

## Out of scope

- Dynamic profile selection ('auto-pick profile based on bead complexity'). Too much magic; explicit profile choice is fine.
- Cost budgets / circuit breakers. FEAT-014 (token awareness) tracks this separately.
- Per-bead profile overrides (bead-level 'requires-profile: smart'). Could be a future follow-up but out of scope here.

## Metadata

Governing spec: FEAT-006 (agent service) + the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641. This bead is the PROFILE-layer policy that sits on top of the routing fixes from ddx-2d974641. Do not start until ddx-2d974641's Phase-1 children are closed.

Fix upstream; bump version when fixed.\n- **omlx server bug**: request is valid and ddx-agent parser is correct, but server returned empty body or malformed SSE frames. .ddx/executions/20260418T015654-977ef255/prompt.md:5:## Problem (re-scoped per fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Running 'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit --max-tier cheap' against an execute-bead prompt fails with:

    agent: provider error: unexpected end of JSON input

after 14.65s. No tokens consumed (input=0, output=0). The session log at .ddx/executions/20260418T005052-3aad2cfa/embedded/*.jsonl shows llm.delta err={Offset: 0} — parser read an empty/malformed first chunk.

## What the bead originally claimed (now disputed)

Original framing declared this an upstream ddx-agent v0.3.11 defect based on 'direct curl against the same endpoint works for non-streaming, tools, and SSE'. Both codex and opus fresh-eyes reviews pushed back that this attribution is not supported by the evidence:

- 'unexpected end of JSON input after 14s is exactly what you'd see if (a) the server closed the stream on an unsupported field, (b) DDx sent stream=true to an omlx flavor that returns a non-SSE 200 with empty body, or (c) the tool schema tripped server-side validation and the server returned {} + close.' — opus
- 'Routing and dispatch still have split control paths... That means malformed provider/model pairing is still possible before the request ever reaches the parser. unexpected end of JSON input is consistent with a broken upstream parser, but also with DDx sending a request shape or streaming mode the omlx server does not fully support.' — codex

## Fix — evidence first, attribution second

This bead now produces wire-level evidence BEFORE concluding where the defect lives. Acceptance criteria are scoped to that.

### Step 1: Capture the wire payload

Reproduce the failure on a host with vidar-omlx reachable and capture the exact HTTP request DDx sends and the exact HTTP response the server returns. Options in ascending order of preference:

a) Run with HTTPS_PROXY pointing at a local mitmproxy or a simple Go tcp-dump proxy that logs request/response bodies + headers to a file.
b) Add a temporary debug flag in ddx-agent (or ddx-side wrapper) to dump the request body + response headers + raw response bytes before parsing.
c) tcpdump/wireshark on the vidar host capturing port 1235, filtering to the DDx attempt's source IP and time window.

### Step 2: Classify the defect

With the wire payload in hand, determine which layer is at fault:

- **DDx bug**: request shape is inconsistent with what omlx (flavor-specific) accepts — e.g., stream=true on a server that returns non-SSE, tool schema shape mismatched, missing/extra header, etc. Fix in DDx (ddx-agent config or ddx-agent library usage).
- **ddx-agent bug**: request is valid and server returned valid SSE/JSON, but agent-lib's parser errored. Fix upstream; bump version when fixed.
- **omlx server bug**: request is valid and ddx-agent parser is correct, but server returned empty body or malformed SSE frames. Fix upstream at ml-explore/mlx_lm; work around in ddx-agent by detecting and retrying with different flags.

### Step 3: File and fix

Whichever of the three paths the evidence points to, file follow-up as needed (a targeted DDx fix, an agent-lib bump, or a workaround). This bead closes when the root cause is identified AND addressed AND a regression test or reproducer exists.

## Evidence captured on eitri 2026-04-17 (pre-scope)

- Host: eitri, DDx binary v0.6.0-alpha22 (commit 93854d30, agent-lib v0.3.11)
- Provider: vidar-omlx (http://vidar:1235/v1)
- Model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit (loaded=true, max_context_window=262144, max_tokens=32768)
- Evidence: .ddx/executions/20260418T005052-3aad2cfa/
- Direct curl WORKS for: non-streaming completion, tools, streaming SSE (session shows all three shapes returned valid responses).

The curl-works isolation is not strong enough to pin the defect upstream — the full execute-bead prompt (system + AGENTS.md + CLAUDE.md + META-PROMPT + bead XML + 6 tool schemas, ~50-100KB wire size) exercises a different code path through ddx-agent than the 3-line curl tests.

## Related catalog-mapping observation (separate issue)

First attempt's escalation hit a catalog-missing model on vidar-omlx (minimax/minimax-m2.7 -> 404). That is covered by ddx-3c5ba7cc (tier-affinity) + ddx-8610020e (RouteRequest.Provider field) + ddx-0486e601 (fuzzy normalization). Not this bead.

## Files likely touched (unknown until step 2)

Depends on classification. Possible surfaces:
- cli/internal/agent/ (if DDx sends a malformed request)
- upstream DocumentDrivenDX/agent (if library parser is broken)
- ml-explore/mlx_lm / omlx server (if server returns empty body)

   430	  expose FEAT-014's routing signal model to UI and MCP consumers
   431	- FEAT-008 (Web UI) — the provider dashboard view is the browser surface for
   432	  FEAT-014's read model; FEAT-014 governs field semantics, unknown-state
   433	  rules, and tooltip registry for that view
   434	- [[MET-001]] (ddx test wall time) — canonical `source: exec` MET artifact for
   435	  projected runtime metrics and evidence formatting
   436	- [[MET-002]] (cost per closed bead) — canonical `source: external` MET artifact
   437	  for process-cost reporting that consumes FEAT-014 usage signals
   438	- provider-native local stores such as `~/.codex/` and `~/.claude/`
   439	- embedded `ddx-agent` runtime telemetry and session references
   440	
   441	## Implementation Strategy
   442	
   443	### Phase 1 — Signal-source spikes
   444	- Inventory provider-native signal sources and freshness semantics
   445	- Define the minimal routing metrics schema and retention rules
   446	  (now part of ddx-agent's internal routing per CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service)
   447	- Resolve whether Claude has a stable non-PTY current-quota source
   448	
   449	### Phase 2 — Preserve native persistence and add adapters
   450	- Remove DDx defaults that suppress codex/claude native persistence

145	---
   146	# Reference: ...
   147	```
   148	
   149	Identity (`id`, `depends_on`, etc.) stays under `ddx:`; type-specific fields
   150	like `source_url`, `source_author`, and `accessed` live at the top level.
   151	
   152	#### Type-specific metadata example: MET
   153	
   154	Metric artifacts (`MET-*`) carry a top-level `metric:` block alongside the
   155	`ddx:` identity block. The block is type-specific metadata that the generic
   156	parser passes through inert; metric tooling (`ddx metric`, `ddx doc validate`)
   157	keys off it.
   158	
   159	```yaml
   160	---
   161	ddx:
   162	  id: MET-007
   163	  depends_on: [FEAT-014, SD-016]
   164	metric:
   165	  schema_version: 1            # required; v1 is the only accepted value
   166	  unit: ms                     # required; free-form (ms, bytes, USD, count, ...)
   167	  direction: lower-is-better   # required; lower-is-better | higher-is-better
   168	  goal: 250                    # optional; aspirational target (documentary)
   169	  budget: 400                  # optional; machine-checkable hard line
   170	  source: exec                 # required; v1 enum: exec | external
   171	  scope: per-attempt           # required; per-attempt | per-bead | per-feature | global
   172	---
   173	# Metric: ...
   174	```
   175	
   176	Field semantics:
   177	
   178	- `schema_version` — pins the metric block schema. v1 is the only accepted
   179	  value; future versions extend rather than replace.
   180	- `unit` — free-form unit string. Mixed-unit history is refused by
   181	  comparison and trend projections (see TD-005).
   182	- `direction` — must match the comparison constants used by the metric
   183	  runtime (`lower-is-better`, `higher-is-better`).
   184	- `goal` vs `budget` — `goal` is aspirational and documentary; `budget`
   185	  is a machine-checkable hard line. When a gate's `thresholds.ratchet`
   186	  observes this metric, the gate ratchet is authoritative for landing
   187	  decisions; `ddx doc validate` emits a warning when `budget` and the
   188	  observing ratchet disagree.
   189	- `source` — `exec` for metrics emitted by `ddx exec` runs; `external`
   190	  for metrics ingested from outside DDx (e.g. cost reports).
   191	- `scope` — declares the granularity at which the value is meaningful.
   192	
   193	`source: derived` is intentionally out of v1; it returns when a real
   194	derivation schema exists. `MET-*` artifacts are project-local; multi-node
   195	aggregation is deferred to a future MET v2.
   196	
   197	> **MET v2 backlog (informational):** derived-source metrics with a real
   198	> formula schema, multi-node aggregation semantics, structured acceptance-
   199	> criteria comparators, and per-MET dashboard wiring are deferred. None of
   200	> these are required by v1; they are tracked here so future work can pick
   201	> them up without rediscovering the shape.
   202	
   203	#### `media_type`
   204	
   205	Identifies how an artifact's bytes should be interpreted. Examples:

100	  and per-bead breakdown
   101	
   102	### US-161: Team Lead Reviews Rework Rate
   103	**As a** team lead planning the next sprint
   104	**I want** to see which features had the most rework
   105	**So that** I can identify areas needing better specs or design
   106	
   107	**Acceptance Criteria:**
   108	- Given beads that were reopened, when I run `ddx metrics rework --since 7d`,
   109	  then I see reopen rate and the specific beads that were reopened
   110	
   111	## Dependencies
   112	
   113	- FEAT-004 (beads) — data source
   114	- FEAT-014 (token awareness) — agent session cost data
   115	- [[MET-002]] (cost per closed bead) — canonical `source: external` MET artifact
   116	  for the per-bead cost reporting shape used by this feature
   117	
   118	## Out of Scope
   119	
   120	- Operational metrics (code performance, API health) — that's FEAT-010
   121	- Custom metric definitions — plugins can build on this data via hooks
   122	- Historical trend storage — metrics are computed on demand from raw data

I’m also running the doc audit now to see whether the second acceptance criterion is already satisfied or blocked by existing repo drift.
